Revise the Notes to Financial Statements to reflect the changes made in the SFPer.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to comply with the disclosure requirements of the IPSAS and the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for LGUs. Specifically, the Financial Statements should include: (1) concise narratives explaining significant changes; (2) disclosures on unreconciled accounts; and (3) explanatory notes on reconciling items. These enhancements will ensure that relevant and reliable information is provided to the intended users of the Financial Statements.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:
Enjoin all municipal officers and employees to adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002, particularly on limiting the granting of cash advance to allowable transactions stated therein to avoid any risk of possible misapplication or misuse of local government funds.
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Issue Demand Letters to Accountable Officers and Employees who have not liquidated their cash advances within the prescribed period as provided in COA Circular No. 97-002 and enforce immediate liquidation of cash advances; and
Withhold the salaries and other emoluments in full of personnel with unliquidated cash advance due to their failure to liquidate their cash advance within the prescribed period as provided under Paragraphs 9.2 and 9.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002.
Direct the concerned incumbent municipal official to settle immediately the unliquidated cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to implement stringent measures to prevent the accumulation of unremitted collections. Specifically, a strict remittance system should be enforced to ensure that all collections are duly remitted by the end of each business day, including those received on Fridays, in strict compliance with Section 29 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I. This measure is necessary to mitigate the risk of public funds being exposed to misappropriation, fraud, or unauthorized use, as well as to prevent delays in the timely utilization of government resources.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to submit the DTRs and duly signed payrolls for CY 2024.  In the absence of signed payrolls, bank documents evidencing the crediting of salaries to the rightful recipients should be submitted in their place.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Accountant to submit the Disbursement Vouchers (DVs) for the 2024 payments to PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG, along with the complete supporting documents as discussed above.
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all disbursements are processed through a duly accomplished DV or Payroll, regardless of whether payment is made by check or through electronic means.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Local Development Council to meticulously plan and evaluate the feasibility of each PPA included in the Annual and Investment Programs to ensure that these PPAs are procurement-and-implementation ready for efficient execution.

We recommended that Management align the utilization of the Special Education Fund (SEF) by ensuring that all disbursements adhere strictly to the allowable purposes outlined under Section 272 of Republic Act No. 7160 and Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017.  To continue supporting college-level scholarship programs, Management may consider identifying alternative and appropriate funding sources, such as the General Fund, in accordance with applicable budgeting regulations.
In addition, the Local School Board is encouraged to carefully review and approve only those SEF-funded Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs) that are consistent with the intent and legal parameters of the SEF. This will help promote fiscal responsibility, and ensure that resources are effectively directed toward improving basic education services.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC, through its Secretariat, ensure that the ABC is clearly indicated in all Request for Quotation (RFQ) forms issued to prospective suppliers, in compliance with the Guidelines for Shopping and Small Value Procurement.
To strengthen compliance and procurement efficiency, it is further recommended that the BAC members and the Secretariat participate in Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB)-accredited seminars and capacity-building activities. These should focus on recent updates to the Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA No. 9184 and related issuances to promote familiarity with applicable procurement standards and best practices.
Management is likewise advised to ensure that all Purchase Orders (POs) are properly and completely filled out prior to issuance, particularly the Place and Date of Delivery and Delivery Terms (e.g., FOB Shipping Point or FOB Destination). The BAC Secretariat and the Accounting Office should implement a review and verification process to check the completeness and correctness of POs, thereby reinforcing internal controls and ensuring conformity with procurement regulations.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
The Human Resource and Management Office cease the practice of including Traveling Expenses in the salary payroll and ensure that all future travel claims are processed as separate disbursement transactions;
The Municipal Accountant to require submission of the following documents before processing any travel-related disbursement:
Approved Travel Order
Itinerary of Travel
Certificate of Travel Completed
Certificate of Appearance or equivalent documentation
The Accounting Office and Human Resource and Management Office establish a checklist-based review system for all travel claims to ensure full compliance with RA No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.
For disbursements already made, the concerned offices must be directed to submit the lacking supporting documents to validate the claims.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to implement the following:
Ensure the regular updating of cashbooks to accurately reflect all collections and deposits, thereby maintaining precise and transparent financial records;
Strictly enforce proper fund allocation, ensuring that all collections are deposited into the specific fund(s) to which they lawfully belong; and
Implement corrective adjustments for deposit errors by making the necessary fund transfers when deposits are credited to the wrong account, rather than employing setoffs or inter-fund adjustments to rectify misallocations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point System to conduct a gender analysis prior to the preparation of the GAD Plan and Budget and ensure the generation and use of sex-disaggregated data to accurately identify and prioritize gender issues. This will help prevent the misclassification of general services as GAD-related activities and promote the implementation of programs that meaningfully address gender disparities, thereby enhancing the impact and compliance of the Municipality’s GAD initiatives.

We recommended that Management reconcile the Due to GSIS account by conducting a detailed review of journal entries and validating them against the subsidiary ledgers (SLs) to ensure that all mispostings or discrepancies are promptly identified and corrected. To enhance the accuracy, completeness, and reliability of withholding and remittance records, internal controls over the recording, monitoring, and reconciliation processes should also be reviewed and strengthened.
Specifically, Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) should be adequately supported by, or clearly referenced to, pertinent source documents such as payroll registers, remittance reports, or GSIS billing statements to ensure a reliable audit trail and facilitate efficient review. Moreover, monthly recapitulations in the journals should be regularly reconciled with the corresponding entries in the SLs to promptly identify and address any discrepancies.
Furthermore, to address possible instances of over-remittance, under-withholding, or misapplication of GSIS payments, we recommend that the Accounting Office coordinate closely with the HRMO to reconcile individual employee accounts. This may be facilitated by utilizing the GSIS Electronic Billing and Collection System (eBCS), which contains detailed records of billed and paid contributions for each member.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the HRMO in utilizing the PhilHealth Electronic Premium Remittance System (ePRS) to reconcile employee contributions with actual remittances. This coordinated effort will help reduce, if not eliminate, the negative balance in the Due to PhilHealth account.

A. Incomplete RPCPPE reports covered only a total of ₱626,618,457.28 or 57.89% of the total PPE, inconsistent with Section 124 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs), Volume I.

We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the following:

For the Inventory Committee to complete the physical inventory count and prepare the RPCPPE and also to submit the report to the relevant offices within the prescribed period; and

For the Inventory Committee and Accounting office to reconcile the differences, between the physical inventory report and the general ledger balance of each PPE account; 

For the Accountant to identify if the difference represents PPE that are still existing, no longer existing but with pertinent records, or no longer existing and without pertinent records. The conduct of property disposal shall be coursed through the guidelines/procedures under COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020. 

B. Non-reconciliation of PPE ledger cards by the Accounting Office and property cards by the GSO, contrary to pertinent provisions of Sections 119 and 120 of Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I

We have recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require:

The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal GSO to reconcile the PPE ledger cards and property cards periodically; and 

C. PPE with an aggregate amount of ₱19,530,887.83 were not found on the supposed location/official station during the physical inventory count, contrary to the provisions of Section 21 and 50 of PD No. 526 and Executive Order (EO) No. 292.

To device, implement, and strengthen internal controls in the handling and recording procedures of PPEs including completion of physical inventory count, issuance and updating of PARs and AREs, preparation of RCPPE and RPCI, periodic reconciliation of records, and property labeling/ tagging. 

Mobilize additional competent personnel from each department to facilitate/ lend help to the Inventory Committee for the completion of physical count.

To present to the Inventory Committee the PPEs that were not found on site in order to inspect and verify their existence and condition and test them for impairment as deemed necessary.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended the management the following:

For the Accounting office to prepare and maintain subsidiary ledger for each kind of PPE owned by the municipality;

For the Inventory Committee to complete the physical inventory count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant, and Equipment (RPCPPE); and also require the submission of the report to the requiring offices within the prescribed period; and

For the Inventory Committee and Accounting office to reconcile the differences, if any, between the physical inventory report and the general ledger balance of each PPE account; subsequently, the Accountant shall identify if the difference represents PPE that are still existing, no longer existing but with pertinent records, or no longer existing and without pertinent records. The conduct of property disposal shall be coursed thru the guidelines/procedures under COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020. 

We have recommended the management to require the Municipal Accountant to maintain property, plant and equipment cards and to reconcile with the property cards of the Municipal GSO.  We also recommend the Municipal GSO to conduct inventory validating the existence of the PPEs still listed in the records of Municipal Accountant.

We have recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the General Services Officer to update/ renew each Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) / Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) every three years after issue or every time there is a change in accountability in accordance with GAAM Volume I.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 be strictly complied. Moreover, require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network. Likewise, require the General Services Officer to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components. And, to create an inventory committee to conduct the physical count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System.

observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 11, Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended the management to adhere with the provisions of COA Circular 2020-006 on the disposition procedures for non-existing/ missing Property, Plant, and Equipment:

•	To conduct investigation to determine the last known location/ office of the missing PPE, as well as the official/ employee accountable for it;

•	To file the request for authority to derecognize non-existing/ missing PPE:

a. if the PPE has already exceeded its estimated useful life supported with certification from Head of Accounting Unit that the missing PPE had already exceeded the useful life.

b. if the accountability/ responsibility over the missing PPE could not be pinpointed after the conduct of investigation, supported with the List of Non-existing/ Missing PPEs with carrying values certified by the Head of the Property Unit and Accounting Unit, approved by the Agency Head and certified copy of the report of investigation.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended the management the following:

For the Sangguniang Bayan Members to forward all Request Letters to the Office of the Local Chief Executive (LCE) as donation is not within their mandated duties and functions;

For the LCE to formulate guidelines and internal control policies centralizing and standardizing the grant of donations. For individual recipients, their Request Letters shall be forwarded to the Municipal Social Development Office (MSWDO) to assess their eligibility. For barangays, ensure that the donations shall be utilized solely for public purpose. Demand supporting documents, such as project proposals, design plans, technical specifications, etc., in cases of requests for construction materials. The construction materials and/or inventories donated to barangays must be received by the them with Sangguniang Barangay Resolution authorizing the barangay to receive the donations, copies of which will form part of the supporting documents of disbursement vouchers to be prepared by the LGU. 

For the Municipal Accountant to update the books of accounts of the barangays for all donations recorded as given by the municipality, and to review the completeness of supporting documents.

For the BAC to ensure that all procurements are done thru public bidding or alternative methods of procurements as the case may be pursuant to RA No. 9184, in order to ensure that the government and the people get maximum benefits and quality of service from the contracts. Compliance to the requirements of public bidding calls for transparency of government transactions and accountability of public officers.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 29, Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended the management the following:
For the Accounting office to prepare and maintain subsidiary ledger for each kind of PPE owned by the municipality;
For the GSO to complete the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant, and Equipment (RPCPPE) and require the submission of the report to the requiring offices within the prescribed period; and
For the GSO and Accounting office to coordinate and reconcile the differences between the physical inventory report and the general ledger balance of each PPE account; which will then be the basis of the Municipal Accountant to make appropriate adjustments to reflect the correct balances of each PPE account.

We have recommended the Municipal Accountant to provide depreciation expense on the following PPE mentioned above in order to correctly present their balances in the financial statements in consonance with IPSAS. Furthermore, the Local Chief Executive to create an appraisal committee task to determine the useful life and appraise value of the above PPE as a basis for the computation of depreciation expense by the Municipal Accountant.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and OIC-Municipal Treasurer to maintain property, plant and equipment cards reconciled with the property cards of the OIC-Municipal Treasurer.

We have recommended that Local Chief Executive require the General Services Officer to update/ renew each Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) / Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) every three years after issue or every time there is a change in accountability in accordance with GAAM Volume I.

We have recommended that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Roads Asset Management System prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015 be strictly complied. Thus, require the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network. Likewise, require the General Services Officer to maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components. Moreover, create an inventory committee to conduct the physical count and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System.

We have recommended that the OIC-Municipal Treasurer deposit all collections daily or not later than the next banking day as provided by the above-stated accounting rules and regulations to avoid possible loss or misappropriation of government funds.

We have recommended the management to deposit all collections daily and intact pursuant to the provisions of Section 69 of PD 1445 and Section 32 of Manual on New Government Accounting System Volume I, and regularly update the cashbooks to reflect the exact amount of collections and deposits. We have further recommended the Liquidating Officer to update and transmit to the OIC-Municipal Treasurer the report of remittances made by the collectors daily.

We have recommended that the OIC-Municipal Treasurer instruct the Petty Cash Fund Custodian to fully liquidate the PCF at the end of each year and a new Cash Advance for the fund shall be set up in the ensuing year.

We have recommended the management the following:
for the Treasury office to devise plans and strategies on how to facilitate the preparation of Certified List of Taxpayers; and coordinate their records with Municipal Assessor;
subsequently, a report on RPT delinquencies must be prepared by the Treasury office and impose penalties and avail remedies set by laws to compel the delinquent taxpayers to pay their taxes on time; and
submit the Certified List of Taxpayers to the Municipal Accountant to determine the accurate amount for the set-up of RPT and SET receivables at the beginning of the year.

We have recommended the Local Chief Executive to enjoin the Accounting office to record the inventoriable purchases following the perpetual inventory method pursuant to the provisions of Section 114 of Manual on New Government Accounting System and subsequently record the corresponding expense accounts only upon distribution. As for the items being forwarded to the RHU and Infirmary, it is recommended that a distribution report be forwarded to the Accounting unit to effect the recording of expense and adjust the inventory accounts and for the Municipal Accountant to ensure that capitalizable items are correctly recorded to their respective asset accounts to in order to reflect accurate balances of PPE accounts.

We have recommended the Municipal Accountant and the Budget Officer to use proper budgetary and expense accounts to avoid improper use of Other Maintenance and Operating Expense account in order to present the correct amount of the specific account in the financial statements.

We have recommended the Management to instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries, to comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-004 dated September 30, 2016 and for proper presentation of LGU Fund balances in the Financial Statements.

We have recommended the Local Chief Executive to:
Enjoin the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to design and implement projects that are for socio-economic and environmental development that partake the nature of investment or capital expenditure pursuant to the mandate of the DILG DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-01 dated February 22, 2017;
For the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to use proper budgetary and asset accounts for the transactions involving 20% Development Fund to avoid overstatement of expense accounts; and
For the management to prepare a comprehensive Annual Investment Plan (AIP) and make revisions as necessary should there be any request from the barangays for developmental projects.

We have recommended the following:
For the LDRRM Officer to prepare and submit promptly to the Accounting Office the report on the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Utilization as of yearend in order to determine the unspent portion to be transferred to Trust Liability – DRRM;
For the Accounting Office to transfer all unexpended/ unobligated balance of the QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE shall be transferred to the Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability-DRRM” in the Trust Fund books pursuant to COA Circular 2012-002 and to maintain corresponding subsidiary ledger; and
For the Accounting Office to disclose in their Notes to Financial Statements the balances and details of the Trust Liability – DRRM for each year. It must be noted that any unutilized amount after five years shall be reverted back to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund and shall be made available for other social services after subsequent enactment by the local sanggunian.

We have recommended that the management avoid reimbursement as a mode of procurement and adopt public bidding or other alternative modes of procurement provided under RA 9184, in order to promote transparency and to avoid possible source of irregularities or corruption. Furthermore, the Municipal Accountant must comply with the Revenue Regulations set by the BIR with respect to withholding of taxes to avoid possible tax penalties which might incurred by the municipality.

We have recommended that the municipality refrain from indicating in the Purchase Request the specific brand name when purchasing equipment, unless pursuant to the exception stated in Section 18 of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184. We further recommend the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to ensure that the procurements of the entity are adherent to the provisions of R.A. 9184 and its IRR.

We have recommended that the management ensure that the utilization of Special Education Fund (SEF) is only made within the priority expenses provided under Sections 100 (c) of Republic Act No. 7160, and DepED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2017 in order to attain the purpose of the fund, to avoid suspension/ disallowance in audit.

We have recommended the management to refrain from granting financial assistance and/or donations in kind to private schools/ institutions in accordance with Section 335 of Republic Act No. 7160 and DepED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1 s. 2017. The utilization of SEF must address the needs of public schools’ operations and maintenance.

We have recommended the GAD Focal Person to prepare a comprehensive GAD Plan and Budget, and for the management to allocate at least 5% of the total budget appropriation for the implementation of GAD-related activities in accordance with Section 36 of Republic Act No. 7910.

We have recommended the Members of the Sanggauniang Bayan to immediately stop the practice of granting assistances/donations to their constituents as this is not among their mandated duties and functions set forth by Section 447 (a) of RA No. 7160 to avoid suspension/ disallowance in audit.

We have recommended the management to stop granting financial assistances to private and religious sectors as this is in violation to Sec 335 of Local Government Code of 1991. We have further recommended the management to demand from barangays and public schools, the Accountable Form No. 51 to properly account for financial assistances they granted to assure that these are properly accounted for.

We recommend the Local Chief Executive to:
Require the Human Resource Management Office to devise policies and guidelines in hiring of employees, especially that of contractual or job orders. Specific duties and responsibilities as required under each department/office must be identified, and the assignment of the applicants must be meticulously determined such that their skills matches the requirement of the department/office;
Basing from the policies and guidelines, the contract of service for each applicant must cite the duties and responsibilities, as well as the period within which to perform such activities; and
Ensure that Monthly Accomplishment reports are prepared and attached with the payroll of job orders.

We have recommended the following:
OIC-Municipal Treasurer to ensure that duly approved DTRs and application for leaves are attached to the payrolls before payment of salaries and wages are made ;
HRMO to verify the DTRs and application for leaves and should ensure that before the DTRs are forwarded to the Local Chief Executive for approval, the DTR should have been processed/ verified and initialed by the concerned Department Head ; and
Municipal Accountant should see to it that supporting documents were complete before recording the transaction in the books of accounts.

We have recommended that OIC-Municipal Treasurer should not pay any disbursement not bearing the required approval of the proper officials in order to ensure the validity and propriety of the transactions, to avoid disallowance in audit.

We have recommended the management to preclude Officials assigned with government service vehicles from receiving transportation allowance in compliance with the provisions of Item 8.2.1 of Local Budget Circular No. 103, to avoid disallowance in audit.

We have recommended that the MSWDO prepare and submit the list of positions in its plantilla with the corresponding job description/ actual duties that describes the basis for justifying such positions who are exposed to occupational risks and assigned to hazardous places as stipulated in DBM and DSWD Joint Circular No. 1, s. 201, and cause the approval of such list by the Local Chief Executive (LCE); and attached to the payroll for hazard pay copy of the DTR and approved leave of absence as certified by the Human Resource Management Officer, if any. Moreover, establish procedures in identifying the personnel assigned to hazardous areas and exposed to occupational risk and duration of their exposure and thereafter it for approval of the LCE.

We have recommended the management to stop the granting of monthly allowance to the Election Officer, and other national government officials, if any, who are not included under Section 2.0 of the Compensation Policy Guidelines No. 98-1 to avoid the incurrence of irregular expenditure.

We have recommended the management to stop the reimbursement of gasoline/fuel expenses to officials who are receiving Transportation Allowance as this construe double compensation which is contrary to Section 8, Article IX-B of the Constitution, which is further classified as an illegal expenditure according to COA Circular 2012-003.

We have recommended the management to strengthen the internal control system with regard to monitoring of gasoline/ fuel expenses to ensure that only the government-owned vehicles charge such expenses. Further, we recommend the management to stop allowing the reimbursement/ payment of gasoline/ fuel expenses to private vehicles as such is considered illegal according to COA Circular 2012-003 pursuant to COA Circular 96-004.

We have recommended the management to strengthen their internal control system in fuel consumption/ gasoline expenses of government vehicles by accomplishing properly the Driver’s Trip Tickets, Requisition and Issue Voucher, and preparing Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption pursuant to COA Circular No. 77-61 also known as the Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Motor Vehicles.

We have recommended the management to refrain from charging to Petty Cash the expenses that are recurring and not petty for payment of maintenance and other operating expenses in adherence to Section 48, Volume I of the NGAS Manual for LGUs and Section 4.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.
We have recommended that Accounting Office reconstruct the maintenance/ repair record of each equipment/ vehicle owned and/or operated by the Municipal Government.

We have recommended the Municipal Officials to:
Economically, efficiently and effectively utilize the resources of the Municipality by discouraging and preventing unnecessary expenditures to avoid suspension or disallowance in audit ; and
To purchase essential items needed in the day to day operations of their respective Municipal Offices to prevent wastage of government funds.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Human Resource Management Officer enjoin all employees of the municipality to comply with the requirements set forth under Section 52 of the General Appropriations Act 2019 and to decline requests from employees for additional loans if such would reduce the net take home pay below minimum; and to adopt control measures to ensure that the employee’s monthly net take home pay not be lower than the minimum amount set by the GAA for the year after deducting mandatory contributions and other obligations.

We have reiterated the previous recommendation for the Municipal Accountant and OIC-Municipal Treasurer, together with the accountable officers concerned, to strictly comply with guidelines requiring the timely preparation of reports and submission of accounts. In effect, the establishment of the legitimacy/validity of transactions could also be determined on a timely basis and avoid inconveniencies on the part of those responsible which will include among others the withholding of salaries for their non-compliance.

We have recommended the management to formulate an action plan to implement the audit recommendations, and submit immediately the duly accomplished AAPSI form to the Audit Team, copy furnished the other oversight government bodies pursuant to Section 99 of Republic Act No. 11260.

We have recommended that Management revise/ update the Municipal Revenue Code of 2003 pursuant to Section 191 of RA No. 7160 to increase the revenue collection of local taxes to finance more valuable projects.

We have recommended that:

Management strictly comply with the guidelines as regards monthly BRS to be regularly and timely prepared for all accounts, in order that the necessary adjustments/corrections could be effected for a more accurate and reliable presentation of the Cash in Bank Account-LCCA;

Management exhaust all possible means in determining the nature of the reconciling items in order to effect immediately the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to present fairly the balance of the Cash in Bank – LCCA account in the financial statements.

For the Treasurer to inform through a letter, payees to whom those checks which are long outstanding had been issued to effect the necessary corrections/adjustments and also to dispel the doubts on the existence of legitimate/valid obligations. 

For the Treasury office to present her records of checks encashed from collections and cancelled checks if any, to the Accounting office to make the necessary entries to adjust the book balances. 

For the Treasurer and Accountant to regularly reconcile their cashbook and ledger balances.

observations.managementAction: The Accounting office has made necessary adjusting entries. It was further disclosed that the minor difference was due to bank charges and interest income; which are to be adjusted.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated the previous year’s recommendation that the Assistant Municipal Treasurer strictly adhere with Section 312 of the Local Government Code and Section 67 of PD 1445 by not allowing accommodation of private checks so that personal monies/ funds are separated from the local funds to avoid negative balance of Cash in Treasury account and to properly monitor whether daily collections are deposited intact.
observations.managementAction: No action was taken to address this deficiency.
observations.reason: Reiterated/Discussed in Finding No. 4, Page 37.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require her collecting agents to prepare and submit daily the Report of Collections and Deposits which will be the basis for recording in the Cashbook and General Ledger. Reconciliation between these two books of transactions be done regularly for monitoring purposes and prevention of financial errors and fraud.
observations.reason: Included in Finding No. 6, Page 43.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Assistant Municipal Treasurer to refrain from encashment of checks in order to have a sound internal control over Cash in Treasury. Moreover, check encashment must be made to the Depository Bank and not to the Treasury Office using the daily collections. Finally, all checks be crossed for deposit to the payee’s account, except checks which are payable to municipal employees.
observations.reason: Discussed in Finding No. 6, Page 43.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Treasurer to send written notice to the payees of the existence of the stale checks. Accordingly, require the Municipal Accountant to make necessary adjustments in the books for the stale checks in order to reflect the correct cash balances.
observations.reason: Discussed in Finding No. 6, Page 43.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we have recommended the following: 

Stop designating JO’s who render administrative works due to existence of regular employees in the agency who is capable and available of doing such work functions and responsibilities.

Require all JO’s to enroll in the biometric system of the Municipality to ensure compliance with the required number of hours of work.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor to cause the immediate relief of: (1) Ms. Shiela Marie M. Castillo; (2) Mr. Reynaldo E. Perez; and (3) Ms. Anniedith Noelle N. Salva as an Accountable Officer of this Municipality, for they have grossly manifested an act that is punishable under Article 217 of the Revised Penal Code and also an act of Dishonesty classified as grave offense under Civil Service Rules punishable by immediate dismissal from government service on the first offense. On the other hand, the Local Chief Executive shall issue a Reprimand Order with respect to Ms. Carlyn C. Quilas and Ms. Elizabeth O. Ubaldo for committing such acts and the two of them shall make a promise not to commit the same offense again or else it would also be the basis for their relief as Accountable Officer.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly adhere with the above cited provisions governing collections and deposits of government funds. Moreover, collections are to be deposited intact not later than the next banking day in order to safeguard funds from the risk of loss, misapplication or misused.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require all Revenue Collecting Personnel to remit their collections at the close of each business day pursuant to Section 29 of the Manual of NGAS, Volume I. Furthermore, collections coming from the Real Property Tax must be closely and stringently monitored to avoid late remittance which could possibly result to misuse or loss of public funds.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require all her collecting agents to prepare and submit daily the Report of Collections and Deposits which shall be the basis for the recording by the Municipal Accountant in the General Ledger.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer reconcile regularly the balances  appearing in the Cashbook and General Ledger for monitoring purposes and prevention of financial errors and fraud.

We have recommended that the Municipal School Board limit the utilization of  SEF only to  programs and projects defined under RA 7160 and DECS, DBM and DILG Joint Circular No. 01 in order to maximize the benefits that the students and teachers could avail under this fund.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe Section 5.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-01 which enumerates the required supporting documents for the payment of Terminal Leave Benefits. Likewise, we encouraged Former Councilors Romeo L. Sim and Reynaldo S. Oligan to surrender all the municipal properties under their accountability so that they could not be held liable for Malversation of Public Property provided under Article 217 of the RPC.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant be strict in the granting of Cash Advance and observe those allowable transactions that can only be availed of thru cash advance pursuant to COA Circular 97-002.

We have recommended that the OIC Municipal Budget Officer strictly adhere to Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 of the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG by avoiding those non-related disaster expenditures to be charged in the LDRRM Fund. Likewise, she must appropriate MOOE in the Office of the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management in order that all operating expenses such as office supplies, repair and maintenance and telephone subscription be charged to the MOOE and not in the LDRRM Fund.

We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant examine the completeness of the supporting documents in the payment of payroll for Job Orders. In case of incomplete supporting documents, the Municipal Accountant is obliged to return the payroll to the HRMO for completion. In addition, the HRMO shall prepare and submit the Contract of Service of all Job Orders for proper review by the CSC pursuant to CSC Resolution No. 020790.
We have recommended that Management exert utmost efforts to meet the demands of timely reporting and submission of accounts.  This is to avoid undue delays that preclude the timely conduct of audit.

We have recommended that the OIC Municipal Budget Officer refrain from accommodating expenditures even in the absence sufficient income from SEF. Likewise, she should limit the utilization of SEF only to programs and projects allowed by existing laws, rules and regulations. Finally, the Municipal Treasurer is encouraged to intensify their collections in SEF to meet the budgeted expenditures of the municipality.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor and Budget Officer continue provide additional funds in order to put into operation the remaining lamp post for the safe and comfortable travel of commuters passing the major streets of the Municipality and to prevent waste of government resources putting the lamp post project expose to wear and tear, obsolescence, and damage.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer stricly implement the provisions of Section 171 of the Local Government Code in the examination of Books of Accounts and pertinent records of business establishments in order to have an assurance of truthful declaration of Gross Sales/ Receipts. As a result, it will give way for a truthful and just declaration that will eventually increase the income of the Municipality to finance its priority projects.

We have recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan pass an ordinance to update the existing Revenue Code and to provide budget for its enactment and publication in order to have a basis in collecting appropriate taxes and revenues to finance priority projects and programs of the municipality.

We have recommended that the management:

a. Reconstitute Inventory Committee which  also include  a representative from the Accounting  Office   in  conducting  a  complete  physical  inventory  of all  PPE. 

b. Require the General Services Officer and the Municipal Accountant to prepare   and maintain an updated property ledger cards and direct the Municipal   Accountant to set-up an effective system by which their PPE records would reconcile with the report on the physical inventory on PPE
observations.managementAction: The General Services Office (GSO) conducted physical inventory on PPE last November 2016, however, no reconciliation was made.
observations.reason:
Audit Observation No. 1

As indicated in the Report on Physical 
Inventory on Property, Plant and Equipment as of December 31, 2016, the amount of un-reconciled PPE balance between the Offices of Accounting and GSO Records amounts to 
P 117,156,960.99

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the management maximize the utilization of the 20% Municipal Development Fund and that proper planning be made so that all its development projects during the year will be implemented immediately.
observations.managementAction: For calendar year 2016, the management has implemented developmental projects totalling to P 12, 676,654.87.
observations.reason:
Verification of records on the utilization of 20% Development Fund revealed that out of P22,401,514.60 current appropriations, only                                     P  12,676,654.87 or 57% were utilized. 

The audit team shall remind the management to maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor create a group that would undertake a thorough study and evaluation on the actual workloads and manpower requirements of the Municipality for each department/office to minimize government spending. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant requires the submission of SB Resolution / Authorization to support the hiring of job orders.
observations.managementAction: For Calendar Year 2016, the management was able to seek an SB Resolution authorizing the Municipal Mayor to hire Job Orders.
observations.reason:
Audit Observation No. 13


Validation of payroll of Job Order disclosed that the municipality contracted the services of Job Order employees in the amount of                                   P 22,008,637.50 for the year 2016 compared to P 19,752,705.55 during the previous year showing an increase of P 2,255,931.95.

The payroll of these Job Orders were processed even in the absence of complete supporting documents which were discussed in detail in Audit Observation  No.13.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the concerned personnel exert utmost efforts to meet the demands of timely reporting and submission of accounts.
observations.managementAction: Municipal Accountant commits to submit the reports and accounts on time as prescribed by existing auditing rules and regulations.
observations.reason:
Audit Observation No. 14

For the year under audit, the Municipality was not able to render accounts on time, thereby causing delay in the post audit of financial transactions. 

As of December 31, 2016, the municipality was able to submit accounts for the month of September, 2016 only.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant to prepare adjusting journal entry to correct the misclassified accounts and to reflect the correct balances of the accounts affected.  Moreover, in the recording of transactions, the Accountant shall refer with the description of accounts provided under COA Circular No. 2015-09.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed to the audit recommendation and committed to re-classify the said account.
observations.reason: Verification of trial balance submitted by the Municipal Accountant disclosed that the Shortage of former municipal officers and employees previously recorded as Accounts Receivable has already been classified to Due from Officers and Employees account.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the concern municipal officials exercise fiscal restraint in the municipality’s resources and allow expenditures only within the limits of available funds to avoid overdraft. Likewise, management shall employ remedial measures by either intensifying collections or trimming down operational expenses to the minimum requirements to avoid or eliminate overdrafts.
observations.managementAction: The OIC Budget Officer informed the audit team that there is still a budget overdraft for CY 2016; however, it is already minimal.
observations.reason:
The total actual obligations incurred exceed the total appropriation resulting to a budget overdraft of                                     P 500,194.08 during the year 2016.

The audit team to remind the management to exercise due diligence in its disbursement which should be subject to the availability of funds to avoid budget overdraft.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer strictly comply with the above cited provisions in the handling of government funds.  Collections are deposited in full and intact daily or next banking day, to safeguard its funds from the risk of loss or theft and possible misuse.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Treasurer contends that there are cases that collections are not deposited intact due to bulk of collections during the business renewal period in the 1st quarter of the year.
 
Likewise, she informed the audit team that the management already availed the pick-up facility of the Landbank to reduce the undeposited collections.
observations.reason:
Audit Observation No. 8.
During cash examination held last September 14, 2016 revealed that there are still collections that were not deposited intact.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management provide budget appropriation and provision for unclaimed Terminal Leaves and Other Benefits for retired and separated officials and employees in compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-009.
observations.managementAction: Management committed to comply with the audit recommendation.
observations.reason:
As per interview with the Municipal Accountant disclosed that the unclaimed Terminal Leaves and Other Benefits of former Municipal Officials   and Employees were not yet recorded in the books of accounts. 

The Audit Team shall remind the Municipal Accountant and Budget Officer to obligate these unclaimed benefits and to prepare adjusting journal entry to reflect in the Financial Statements

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer release the amount of RPT Share due for barangays as prescribed in Section 271 (d) of the Local Government Code.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer informed the audit team that the 3rd and 4th quarters of RPT Barangay share for CY 2016 were already released during the month of February 2017.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Sanggunian Bayan pass an ordinance to update the existing revenue code and provide budget for its enactment and publicity in order to maximize the collection of revenues, thus it increases the local budget for the delivery of basic services to its constituents.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed and committed to pass an ordinance updating the existing revenue code just after the national and local election of 2016.
observations.reason:
Audit Observation No. 18.

The “Proposed Ordinance Enacting the Updated Municipal Revenue Code of Rosales” has been approved on the first reading and currently undergoing further study.
